My garden is completely overrun with
brambles, and most people just throw

them away. But, in this video, I'm going
to see if I can turn this thorny

nightmare into tea that actually taste
good.

To begin this experiment, I first
started by cutting and removing all of

the brambles and stuck them up into a
pile. Now, we just have to turn this

into tea. I put all of my leaves into
a wooden box so they can start to wilt

and release their moisture.
Now, I'm just turning the leaves around

so they wilt evenly.
The next day, I came back, but something

had happened.
I think we might have just discovered

alone.
And here I have, you know that scent

when you go into a flower shop? That's

So, what's happening here?
We put a big amount of leaves into a

box, and the leaves started to wilt at
the surface, but the bottom leaves were

under a different environment.
>> [music]

>> They had higher humidity, low oxygen,
and pressure from the leaves above them.

Something about these three parameters
permitted incredible aromas to develop.

When the wilting was done and the midrib
could bend without snapping, I put the

leaves in a plastic bag and put them in
the freezer.

24 hours later, I picked the leaves out
of the freezer and let them thaw. The

reason I freeze the leaves is because it
breaks the cell structure of the leaves

and allows for oxidation, which is
exactly what's happening in black tea

[music] making. Now, because we're
dealing with difficult leaves, like they

have thorns on them, so it's hard to
process them.

You can find various way to bruise them
to induce even more oxidation, like I

did here. Then I put some weight on them
and cover with a damp cloth. You have to

let it sit for 6 to 8 hours for it to
fully oxidize. Or there's a hack.

In the 1930s, a British tea technologist
invented a new method to process tea. He

was seeking a simple way to mass produce
tea, and he came up with a method called

CTC, which stands for crush, tear, and
curl. Basically, the leaves are put into

machine and kind of crushed and cut and
[music] and torn apart at the same time,

which induces a really quick and potent
[music]

oxidation. You're probably familiar with
it because that's actually the stuff you

find in regular black tea bags. Now, why
is this technique relevant for us?

Well, we can't really bruise our leaves
by hand because of their thorns, and

therefore we can't really achieve high
oxidation. But we can mimic this CTC

method at home using a simple meat
grinder. By using this, we avoid dealing

with the thorns, and we actually manage
to highly bruise and therefore oxidize

our leaves, which will give us better
flavor transformation at the end. As you

can see here, it already started to
oxidize. Our leaves turned to way

darker. Here again, let it sit for 6 to
8 hours.

When it's done, you could just dehydrate

go even further.

Now, I think we learned a few videos

into kind of a brick. Then, I put this
in the oven, and I roasted it for 20

minutes at 120°
C.

The idea here is to develop a roasty
flavor on the exterior of the brick

whilst preserving the more delicate
floral notes at the center. This way, we

get [music] more complexity out of our
tea.
